disease.name: Benign Biliary Stricture
disease.note: Non-malignant narrowing of the bile duct, most commonly due to iatrogenic injury during cholecystectomy (80%). Other causes: PSC, chronic pancreatitis, anastomotic stricture post-liver transplant, IgG4-related disease, and trauma. Presents with obstructive jaundice, pruritus, cholangitis. Diagnosis requires ruling out malignancy. Endoscopic or surgical management depending on type, location, and cause.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: With cholangitis -> EMERGENCY; High stricture -> URGENT referral; Low stricture -> ELECTIVE

historyFeatures.note: Most common cause; symptoms may present weeks to years after surgery
Often mild; helps distinguish from malignant

examFeatures.note: Portal hypertension if secondary biliary cirrhosis

subtypes.name: Post-Cholecystectomy Biliary Stricture
subtypes.management: Endoscopic dilation + multiple plastic stents (sequential ERCP with increasing number/size of stents over 6-12 months) for low strictures. Hepaticojejunostomy Roux-en-Y for high (Bismuth III-V) strictures or failed endoscopic therapy.

subtypes.name: Chronic Pancreatitis CBD Stricture
subtypes.management: Endoscopic metal stent (FCSEMS) for benign distal stricture. If failed: surgical biliary-enteric bypass (hepaticojejunostomy).

laboratory.name: LFTs (bilirubin, ALP, GGT, ALT, AST)
laboratory.indication: obstructive pattern: elevated conjugated bilirubin, ALP, GGT

laboratory.name: CA 19-9
laboratory.indication: may be elevated even in benign stricture if cholangitis present; difficult to interpret

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound
imaging.indication: dilated bile ducts proximal to stricture; level of obstruction; exclude gallstones

imaging.name: MRCP
imaging.indication: non-invasive: stricture location, length, character (smooth/tapered vs irregular/shouldered); Bismuth classification

imaging.name: ERCP with brushings + FISH
imaging.indication: therapeutic (dilation, stenting) + diagnostic (brush cytology, FISH to exclude malignancy)

imaging.name: CT abdomen
imaging.indication: assess for mass, vascular involvement, chronic pancreatitis

management.protocolReference: ASGE Guidelines for Biliary Strictures; SAGES Guidelines

pathways.criteria: Short segment stricture of CBD or CHD >2cm from hilum; Bismuth I or II
pathways.treatment: ERCP with balloon dilation + multiple plastic stents (sequential ERCP q3-4 months with increasing number/size of stents; total stenting period 6-12 months). Covered self-expanding metal stents (FCSEMS) are alternative with similar efficacy. Success rates: 70-90% with endoscopic therapy. Stricture recurrence: 10-30%.

pathways.criteria: Hilar stricture, Bismuth III-V, or failed endoscopic therapy
pathways.treatment: Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy is definitive. Bile duct reconstruction with mucosal apposition. Percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD) as bridge or if endoscopic inaccessible. Surgical success: >90%. Recurrence: 10-20%.

pathways.criteria: Distal CBD stricture from chronic pancreatitis
pathways.treatment: Endoscopic: FCSEMS (fully covered self-expanding metal stent) for 6-12 months. If failed: surgical biliary bypass (hepaticojejunostomy or choledochoduodenostomy). Treat underlying chronic pancreatitis (pain management, pancreatic enzyme replacement, endoscopic therapy for PD stricture).
